questions.text: All possible magnetic quantum numbers for an electron on the second main energy level
questions.exp: The second main energy level (n=2) has sublevels s (l=0) and p (l=1). The magnetic quantum number m_l ranges from −l to +l. For p (l=1): m_l = −1, 0, 1. Hence all possible values across the second level are −1, 0, 1.

questions.text:
Which of the following is/are false about ionic bond?
(i) It is non-directional
(ii) It forms between metals and non-metals
(iii) Materials with ionic bonds are usually ductile
(iv) Materials with ionic bonds are electrically insulative
questions.exp: Ionic bonds ARE non-directional (i is true) and DO form between metals and non-metals (ii is true). Materials with ionic bonds are brittle, not ductile — so (iii) is FALSE. They are typically electrically insulative (iv is true). Only (iii) is false.

questions.text:
Which of the following is/are correct about the nuclei of atoms?
(i) Every nucleus contains equal numbers of neutrons and protons
(ii) Every nucleus of an element contains the same number of neutrons
(iii) Every nucleus of an element contains the same number of protons, but the number of neutrons may differ
(iv) Neutron number differs from isotope to isotope, as do proton numbers
questions.exp: The atomic number (number of protons) defines an element — all atoms of the same element have the same proton count. However, isotopes of the same element differ in neutron count. So (iii) is correct.

questions.text: Elements in a column in the periodic table have the same ……
questions.exp: Elements in the same group (column) of the periodic table have the same number of valence electrons, which is why they exhibit similar chemical properties.

questions.text: Sodium forms ionic bonds with Chlorine, since Chlorine is:
questions.exp: Chlorine is highly electronegative. It gains an electron from sodium to form a chloride ion (Cl⁻), which is an anion. Sodium, being electropositive, loses an electron to form Na⁺ (a cation).

questions.text: Covalent bonds form between:
opts: Electropositive elements close to each other in the periodic table
Electropositive elements anywhere in the periodic table
Electronegative elements close to each other in the periodic table
Electronegative elements anywhere in the periodic table
Two metals close to each other in the periodic table
questions.exp: Covalent bonds form when electronegative elements (non-metals) that are close to each other in the periodic table share electrons to complete their outer shells, rather than transferring electrons.

questions.text: Possible type(s) of bonding in a transition metal:
questions.exp: Transition metals primarily exhibit metallic bonding (sea of electrons). However, because their d-orbitals are partially filled, they can also form directional covalent bonds. This gives them higher melting points and strength than typical metals.

questions.text: If the principal quantum number for an element is 3, the maximum possible value for its subsidiary (secondary) quantum number would be:
questions.exp: The secondary (azimuthal) quantum number l ranges from 0 to n−1. For n=3, maximum l = 3−1 = 2 (corresponding to the d subshell).

questions.text: Metallic bonds form when:
opts: Atoms of non-metals accept electrons from metallic atoms
Atoms of non-metals share electrons to form complete outer shells
The valence electrons of atoms form a "sea of electrons" that binds the proton cores together
Atoms of different electronegativities in a molecule cause the molecule to form a dipole
None of the above is true
questions.exp: Metallic bonding is described by the electron-sea model: valence electrons are delocalized and form a "sea" that holds the positive ion cores together. This explains metallic properties like conductivity, malleability, and ductility.

questions.exp: P has configuration ending in 3s², indicating it is in Group IIA (like Mg). It has only 2 valence electrons and readily donates them, making it highly electropositive. T is a transition metal (d-block), not simply electropositive.

R (…3d¹⁰4s²4p⁶) has a completely filled 3d subshell and is therefore NOT a transition element by the strict IUPAC definition. Only T qualifies.

questions.exp: P is electropositive (metal-like, donates electrons). For an ionic bond, it needs an electronegative element that accepts electrons. Q (halogen-like, 3s²3p⁵) is highly electronegative and will accept the electron, forming an ionic bond with P.

questions.exp: S has configuration 3s²3p², like silicon — it needs 4 more electrons and can share electrons covalently. Q (halogen, 3s²3p⁵) is electronegative and can share electrons with S. S itself can also form covalent bonds with other S atoms (like Si in SiC-type structures).

questions.text: From the same configurations (P–T above), the element that usually forms van der Waals bonds within its atoms:
questions.exp: R has configuration ending in 3d¹⁰4s²4p⁶ — a full outer shell (noble gas configuration, like Kr). Noble gases have no tendency to form ionic or covalent bonds and interact only through weak van der Waals (London dispersion) forces.

questions.text: From the same configurations (P–T above), the metal that can be used in high-strength applications:
questions.exp: T has a partially filled d shell (like Fe). Transition metals with mixed metallic and covalent bonding (d-orbital contribution) have higher strength, hardness, and melting points, making them suitable for high-strength applications.

questions.text: Covalent bonds form when:
opts: Atoms of non-metals accept electrons from metallic atoms
Atoms of non-metals share electrons to form complete outer shells
Valence electrons form a "sea" that binds proton cores
Atoms of different electronegativities form dipoles
questions.exp: Covalent bonds arise when two non-metal atoms share electrons so that each can achieve a complete outer electron shell (usually 8 electrons — the octet rule). This shared electron pair constitutes the bond.

questions.text: Element Y has electron configuration "…5s²4d¹⁰5p⁶". The type of bond in a material consisting only of Y atoms:
questions.exp: The configuration 5s²4d¹⁰5p⁶ is a complete outer shell — this is a noble gas (like Xenon). Noble gases have no tendency to form chemical bonds and interact only through weak London dispersion (van der Waals) forces.

questions.exp: P has configuration ending in 3s¹ — a single valence electron (like sodium/Na). It very easily loses that one electron, making it the most electropositive. Electropositive character increases as valence electrons decrease and are more easily donated.

questions.text: From configs above (P–T 2012 set), element(s) that can form covalent bonds with S (3s²3p²):
questions.exp: S has 4 valence electrons (like Si) and can share electrons covalently. R (3d¹⁰4s²4p⁵) is halogen-like and can share electrons with S. S can also form covalent bonds with itself. So R and S can both form covalent bonds with S.

questions.exp: T has configuration 3s²3p⁶ — a complete outer shell (noble gas configuration, like Argon). Noble gases interact only through van der Waals forces.

questions.text: From configs above (P–T 2012 set), the metal that can be used in high-strength applications:
questions.exp: Q has configuration …3d⁶4s² — a transition metal (like iron) with a partially filled d shell. The mixed metallic-covalent d-orbital bonding gives transition metals exceptional strength for high-performance applications.

questions.text: The statement(s) NOT included in Bohr theory:
opts: Electrons are restricted to specific energy levels
No two electrons can have all four quantum numbers the same
Electrons move from lower to higher energy levels when receiving energy
Sub-levels with lowest energy are filled first
questions.exp: Bohr theory states (i) and (iii). The Pauli Exclusion Principle (ii) and the Aufbau Principle (iv) are quantum mechanical rules that came after Bohr's model. Both (ii) and (iv) are NOT part of Bohr's original theory.

questions.text: Which of the following bonds are NOT non-directional? (i.e., which is directional)
questions.exp: Ionic, metallic, and van der Waals bonds are non-directional — they act equally in all directions. Covalent bonds are directional because they involve specific orbital overlaps at defined angles (bond angles determined by electron geometry).

questions.text: High carbon steels are:
questions.exp: Increasing carbon content in steel increases hardness through solid solution and carbide formation, but also increases brittleness by hindering dislocation movement. High carbon steel is therefore hard and brittle.

questions.text: The type(s) of bonding available in polymers:
questions.exp: Polymer chains are held together internally by strong covalent bonds (C–C backbone). Between different polymer chains, weak van der Waals (and sometimes hydrogen) bonds act. So polymers have covalent and/or van der Waals bonding.

questions.text: Ceramic materials are:
questions.exp: Ceramics are characterized by strong ionic and/or covalent bonds, making them very hard. However, these bonds resist plastic deformation (dislocation movement is very difficult), making ceramics brittle. They are hard and brittle.

questions.text:
Which combination of quantum numbers is NOT available for an electron?

(i) n=2, l=0, ml=0, ms=-1/2
(ii) n=2, l=2, ml=1, ms=-1/2
(iii) n=3, l=2, ml=1, ms=+1/2
(iv) n=3, l=1, ml=0, ms=+1/2
(v) n=3, l=0, ml=0, ms=+1/2
questions.exp: Combination (ii) is invalid: the secondary quantum number l must satisfy 0 <= l <= n-1. For n=2, l can only be 0 or 1. l=2 when n=2 is impossible. All other combinations obey the quantum number rules.

questions.text: Transition metals like tungsten and molybdenum have bonds which are:
questions.exp: Transition metals exhibit primary metallic bonding (delocalised sea of electrons) but their partially filled d-orbitals also allow directional covalent bonding contributions. This hybrid accounts for their exceptionally high melting points and mechanical strength compared to s-block metals.

questions.text: Which of the following is TRUE?
opts: Cu is a transition metal because its d-orbital is not fully occupied
Intermolecular bonds always decide the melting point and strength of a composite
Glass contains ionic and covalent bonds though it is an amorphous ceramic
Iron has a larger elastic modulus compared with diamond
questions.exp: Silica glass is an amorphous ceramic with a continuous random network of strong covalent Si-O bonds, modified by network ions (Na+, Ca2+) that introduce ionic character. The others are false: Cu has a full 3d10 shell; diamond is far stiffer than iron; amorphous metallic glasses do exist.

questions.text: All possible magnetic quantum numbers for an electron on the second main energy level (n=2) are:
questions.exp: For n=2, l can be 0 or 1. The magnetic quantum number ml ranges from -l to +l. For l=0: ml=0. For l=1: ml = -1, 0, +1. The complete set of all possible ml values at n=2 is {-1, 0, +1}.

questions.imgAlt: Figure 1: Periodic table portion. P=far-left s-block, Q=centre d-block, R=right p-block, S=Group 17 halogen, T=Group 18 noble gas.
questions.context: Reference for Q28-Q31 (2010): Figure 1 periodic table. P=s-block (far left, electropositive metal). Q=d-block (transition metal). R=p-block right (metalloid/non-metal). S=Group 17 (halogen, highly electronegative). T=Group 18 (noble gas).
questions.text: Using Figure 1, which element can form an ionic bond with element P?
questions.exp: P is an s-block electropositive metal that readily loses electrons. For an ionic bond it needs a highly electronegative non-metal to accept those electrons. S is in Group 17 (halogens) - the most electronegative group that forms stable anions, making S the ideal ionic partner for P.

questions.imgAlt: Figure 1: Periodic table portion. P=s-block, Q=d-block, R=p-block, S=Group 17, T=Group 18.
questions.context: Reference for Q28-Q31 (2010): Figure 1 periodic table. P=s-block (far left, electropositive metal). Q=d-block (transition metal). R=p-block right (metalloid/non-metal). S=Group 17 (halogen, highly electronegative). T=Group 18 (noble gas).
questions.text: Using Figure 1, which element(s) can form covalent bonds with element R?
questions.exp: R is in the middle p-block (like C or Si). It can share electrons with itself (R) to form covalent network solids, and it can share electrons with halogens (S) to form covalent molecules. Both R and S are electronegative enough to share rather than fully transfer electrons.

questions.imgAlt: Figure 1: Periodic table portion.
questions.context: Reference for Q28-Q31 (2010): Figure 1 periodic table. P=s-block (far left, electropositive metal). Q=d-block (transition metal). R=p-block right (metalloid/non-metal). S=Group 17 (halogen, highly electronegative). T=Group 18 (noble gas).
questions.text: Using Figure 1, which element usually forms van der Waals bonds between its atoms?
questions.exp: T is in Group 18 (noble gases). With a completely full outer shell it has essentially zero tendency to form primary chemical bonds. The only interactions between its atoms are weak London dispersion (van der Waals) forces.

questions.imgAlt: Figure 1: Periodic table portion.
questions.context: Reference for Q28-Q31 (2010): Figure 1 periodic table. P=s-block (far left, electropositive metal). Q=d-block (transition metal). R=p-block right (metalloid/non-metal). S=Group 17 (halogen, highly electronegative). T=Group 18 (noble gas).
questions.text: Using Figure 1, which metal can be used in high-strength applications?
questions.exp: Q is in the d-block (transition metals). Transition metals use both s and partially filled d-orbital electrons for bonding, producing a hybrid of metallic and covalent bonding that grants superior strength, hardness, and high-temperature performance.

questions.text: Which of the following bonds are directional?
questions.exp: Covalent bonds are highly directional because they result from the overlap of specific electron orbitals, dictating precise bond angles and molecular geometry. Ionic, metallic, and van der Waals bonds exert forces equally in all directions (non-directional).

questions.text: Which of the following is correct according to Bohr's theory?
opts: Electrons of an atom are restricted to specific energy levels
No two electrons in a single atom can have all four quantum numbers the same
When an electron receives energy it moves from a lower to a higher energy level
Sub-levels with the lowest energy are filled before those with higher energy
questions.exp: The core postulate of Bohr's model is that electrons orbit only in discrete, quantised energy levels. Option (ii) is the Pauli Exclusion Principle; option (iv) is the Aufbau Principle — both are modern quantum mechanics rules, not Bohr's original theory.

questions.text: If the principal quantum number is 3, how many possible values are there for the secondary quantum number?
questions.exp: The secondary (azimuthal) quantum number l ranges from 0 to n-1. For n=3, possible values are l=0, 1, 2 — exactly 3 values corresponding to the s, p, and d subshells.

questions.text: Which of the following is/are non-directional bonds?
questions.exp: Ionic bonds act spherically between charged ions (non-directional). Van der Waals forces are also non-directional. Covalent bonds require specific orbital overlap (directional), and hydrogen bonds depend on precise linear dipole alignment (also directional).

questions.text: Transition metals are:
questions.exp: Transition metals are d-block elements (progressively filling d-orbitals). Their partial d-orbital covalent bonding contribution on top of metallic bonding makes them generally stronger, harder, and higher melting than s-block metals.

questions.text: The relative atomic mass of metal X is 60. What is the mass of one atom of X? (Avogadro's number = 6.022 x 10^23 mol^-1)
questions.exp: One mole of X has a mass of 60 g. Mass of one atom = molar mass / Avogadro's number = 60 / (6.022 x 10^23) = 9.96 x 10^-23 g per atom.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a non-directional bond?
questions.exp: The question asks which IS directional. Metallic, ionic, and van der Waals bonds are all non-directional. Hydrogen bonds are directional — their strength depends on near-linear alignment of the hydrogen atom between two electronegative atoms.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a composite material?
questions.exp: A composite combines two or more distinct materials. GFRP (glass fibres in polymer matrix), asphalt mix (bitumen + aggregate), and chip board (wood chips + resin) are all composites. Cement itself is a single-phase ceramic binder — it only becomes a composite when combined with aggregates to form concrete.

questions.text: Stainless steel is:
questions.exp: Stainless steel is a ferrous (iron-based) alloy steel, distinguished from plain carbon steel by significant alloying additions — at minimum 10.5% chromium — which forms a passive oxide layer giving corrosion resistance.

questions.text: "Aluminium is a ceramic material." State whether this is TRUE or FALSE.
questions.exp: Aluminium is a pure metal: metallic bonding, high electrical and thermal conductivity, plastic malleability. Ceramics are inorganic non-metallic solids (like aluminium oxide) with ionic/covalent bonds. The statement is FALSE.

questions.text: Covalent and ionic solids are not suitable for constructional purposes because they:
questions.exp: Although covalent and ionic solids have very strong bonds, their rigid bond structures prevent plastic deformation. Unlike metals where dislocations absorb energy, these materials cannot deform plastically and instead fracture catastrophically — they are brittle.

questions.text: The relative atomic mass of Po is 210. It crystallizes in simple cubic structure with density 9.32 g/cm³. Find the lattice constant of Po. (Avogadro's number = 6.023×10²³ mol⁻¹)
What is the radius of the largest impurity atom that can be placed within the void space at the body-center of an FCC lattice without distorting the arrangement?
opts: Equal to the radius of parent atoms
0.4 times the radius of parent atoms
0.7 times the radius of parent atoms
0.8 times the radius of parent atoms
1.5 times the radius of parent atoms
questions.exp: The octahedral void at the body-center of FCC has a radius ratio r/R = 0.414 ≈ 0.4. So the largest impurity atom that fits without distortion has radius ≈ 0.4R, where R is the parent atom radius.

questions.text: What will be the packing factor of a simple cubic lattice when all the void spaces are occupied by foreign atoms that just fit into those voids?
questions.exp: SC packing factor alone is 0.524. The cubic void at body center of SC has r/R = 0.732. Adding those foreign atoms (which touch the corner atoms) increases the total volume fraction. The combined packing factor comes to approximately 0.561.

questions.text: In a FCC unit cell (lattice parameter a), X is the midpoint of edge UV (so UX=XV). What are the Miller Indices of direction OX?
questions.exp: If O is the origin (0,0,0) and X is the midpoint of an edge like U(0,1,1) to V(1,1,1), X is located at (1/2, 1, 1). The direction vector OX is [1/2 1 1]. To clear the fractions, multiply by 2 to get the Miller indices [1 2 2]. Square brackets denote a direction.

questions.text: A metal has atomic mass 26, cubic structure with lattice parameter 4.0 Å, and density 2.7 g/cm³. Identify its structure. (Avogadro = 6.023×10²³)
questions.exp: n = ρ·Nₐ·a³/M = (2.7 × 6.023×10²³ × (4×10⁻⁸)³)/26 = (2.7 × 6.023×10²³ × 6.4×10⁻²³)/26 ≈ 4 atoms/unit cell. A cubic structure with 4 atoms/cell is FCC (face-centred cubic).

questions.text: Miller indices of a plane intersecting x, y, z axes at a, a/2, a/3 respectively (a = lattice parameter):
questions.exp: Intercepts: 1, 1/2, 1/3. Take reciprocals: 1, 2, 3. Clear fractions (already integers). Miller indices = (1 2 3). Note: plane indices use parentheses ().

questions.text: What volume percentage of an FCC lattice will be void when half the octahedral interstices are occupied by foreign atoms that just fit in?
questions.exp: FCC packing factor is 0.74, meaning 26% is void. An octahedral void has radius r = 0.414R. The volume of one foreign atom is (0.414)³ ≈ 0.071 times a host atom. Filling 2 of the 4 voids adds ~2.6% to the occupied volume (74% + 2.6% = 76.6%). This leaves 100% - 76.6% = 23.4%, or about 23% void space.

questions.text: The ratio of the radius of an octahedral interstice to the radius of the parent atom in an FCC lattice is:
questions.exp: For an octahedral void in FCC, the geometry gives r_void/R_atom = 0.414.

questions.text: What is the planar density of (110) plane of FCC in terms of atomic radius R?
questions.exp: FCC (110) plane has atoms at 4 corners (each 1/4) + 2 face centers on the long edges (each 1/2) = 1+1 = 2 atoms. Area = a × a√2 = a²√2. In FCC, a = 2R√2. Area = (2R√2)²×√2 = 8R²√2 ≈ 11.31R². Planar density = 2/(11.31R²) ≈ 0.177 R⁻².

questions.text: Miller indices of a plane intersecting x, y, z axes at 0.25a, 0.5a, 0.75a (a = lattice parameter):
questions.exp: Intercepts: 1/4, 1/2, 3/4 (in units of a). Reciprocals: 4, 2, 4/3. Multiply by 3 to clear fractions: 12, 6, 4. Miller indices = (12 6 4). Parentheses for planes.

questions.text: Majority of the polymers are __________ in nature.
questions.exp: Most polymers are semi-crystalline — they have both crystalline regions (where polymer chains are regularly ordered) and amorphous regions (randomly coiled chains). Very few polymers are fully crystalline; many technical polymers are semi-crystalline.

questions.text: What volume percentage of a hexagonal close packed (HCP) structure will be usually void?
questions.exp: HCP has the same packing factor as FCC (both are close-packed structures): 74% occupied, 26% void. The arrangement of close-packed layers (ABABAB in HCP vs ABCABC in FCC) achieves the same maximum packing density.

questions.text: A metal changes from FCC to simple cubic on cooling. What happens to the volume if atomic radius change is negligible?
opts: None of the above is true
questions.exp: FCC packing factor = 0.74; SC packing factor = 0.52. If the atoms stay the same size but rearrange from FCC to SC, they pack less efficiently. To maintain the same number of atoms, the volume must increase. FCC→SC transition increases volume.

questions.text: Close packed direction(s) in a BCC structure:
questions.exp: In BCC, atoms touch along the body diagonal. The body diagonal direction is [111]. Along [100] and [110], atoms do NOT touch. Therefore [111] is the only close packed direction in BCC.

questions.text: Close packed direction(s) in an FCC structure:
questions.exp: In FCC, atoms touch along the face diagonal. The face diagonal direction is [110]. Along [100], atoms do NOT touch (gap between them). Along [111] (body diagonal) in FCC, atoms also do not touch. Therefore [110] is the close packed direction in FCC.

questions.text: Close packed plane(s) in a BCC structure:
questions.exp: BCC structures do not have any true close-packed planes (where the planar packing fraction is 0.906). While the (110) plane is the *most* densely packed plane within the BCC system, it does not meet the strict crystallographic definition of a close-packed plane.

questions.text: Close packed plane(s) in an FCC structure:
questions.exp: In FCC, the (111) plane is the close-packed plane — atoms touch along [110] directions within this plane and achieve the maximum planar packing density. FCC and HCP share this (111) close-packed plane characteristic.

questions.text: An interstitial is:
opts: A defect involving several atoms over a finite volume
A pair of point defects when an ion moves leaving a vacancy (Frenkel)
A point defect when an atom is placed at a site not normally a lattice point
A defect from an atom leaving a vacant lattice site (vacancy)
An interface separating two crystal groups of different crystallographic orientation
questions.exp: An interstitial (self-interstitial or interstitial impurity) is a point defect where an atom occupies a position that is not a normal lattice site — i.e., it sits in the space between regular lattice positions.

questions.text: A grain boundary is:
opts: A defect involving several atoms over a finite volume
A point defect at a non-lattice site
An interface separating two crystal groups of different crystallographic orientations
questions.exp: A grain boundary is a planar (2D) defect — specifically an interface that separates two grains (crystals) having different crystallographic orientations. It is classified as a surface/interfacial defect, not a point or line defect.

questions.text: Which plane has the greatest planar density in FCC?
questions.exp: In FCC, the (111) plane is the most densely packed — 2 atoms per unit cell area of a²√3/2, giving planar density = 4/(√3·a²) = 0.2296/R². This is higher than (100) (0.159/R²) or (110) (0.112/R²).

questions.text: Which plane has the greatest planar density in BCC?
questions.exp: In BCC, the (110) plane has the highest planar density. It contains 2 atoms per a²√2 area. This is because the close-packed direction [111] lies within the (110) plane, so atoms are touching within this plane.

questions.text: What volume percentage of a BCC lattice will be usually void?
questions.exp: BCC has an atomic packing factor (APF) of 0.68, meaning 68% of the unit cell volume is occupied by atoms. The remaining 32% is void space.

questions.text: In simple cubic structure, nearest neighbors are at distance a. What is the nearest neighbor distance in BCC?
questions.exp: In BCC, atoms touch along the body diagonal [111]. The body diagonal length = a√3. Each body diagonal connects two corner atoms through the body center, so nearest neighbor distance = (a√3)/2 ≈ 0.866a ≈ 0.9a (exam approximation). More precisely ≈0.866a.

questions.text: What is the nearest neighbor distance in FCC structure?
questions.exp: Wait — in FCC, atoms touch along the face diagonal [110]. Face diagonal = a√2. Nearest neighbor distance = a√2/2 = 0.707a ≈ 0.7a. Same numerical answer as BCC but for a different direction.

questions.text: A group of crystals having a particular crystallographic orientation different from its neighbors is known as:
questions.exp: A grain is defined as a region (group of unit cells) all having the same crystallographic orientation. Different grains are separated by grain boundaries. A polycrystalline material consists of many grains.

questions.text: What is the shortest distance between two atoms of a close-packed cubic lattice of lattice parameter a?
questions.exp: A close-packed cubic lattice is FCC. Atoms touch along the face diagonal: 4R = a√2, so 2R = a√2/2 = a/√2. The shortest distance between atom centers = 2R = a/√2.

questions.text: Which of the following planes is/are close packed?
questions.exp: Strictly speaking, only the (111) planes in FCC and the basal planes in HCP are true close-packed planes (with an atomic packing fraction of 0.906). The (110) plane in BCC is its *most densely* packed plane, but it is not a true close-packed plane because the atoms do not achieve maximum 2D packing. Therefore, none of the options are true close-packed planes.

questions.text: Which of the following directions is/are close packed?
questions.exp: [110] of FCC: atoms touch along the face diagonal — YES, close packed. [110] of BCC: atoms do NOT touch along this direction — NO. [100] of BCC: not close packed. [110] of SC: atoms don't touch. Therefore only [110] of FCC is close packed among these options.

questions.text: Iron changes from BCC to FCC structure on heating above 910°C. Such behavior of iron is called:
questions.exp: When a solid element can exist in more than one crystal structure depending on temperature or pressure, it is called allotropy (or polymorphism for compounds). Iron's α (BCC) to γ (FCC) transition at 910°C is a classic example of allotropy.

questions.text: What happens to the volume of iron during the BCC→FCC transition on heating above 910°C?
opts: Depends on atomic radii before and after
Remains same only if atomic radius unchanged
questions.exp: The volume change depends on both the change in atomic radius and packing efficiency. BCC has APF = 0.68 and FCC has APF = 0.74. If the atomic radius were unchanged, the denser FCC packing would reduce volume. However, the atomic radius itself changes during the BCC→FCC transition — and if the radius increases enough, the volume can actually increase despite the higher packing efficiency. For example, if the radius grows from a smaller BCC value to a noticeably larger FCC value, the extra space each atom occupies can outweigh the gain in packing efficiency, resulting in a net volume increase. So the outcome is not fixed: it depends on the actual atomic radii before and after the transition.

questions.text: An FCC lattice and a BCC lattice have the same lattice parameter. Which quantity will also be the same?
opts: Number of atoms per unit cell
Volume of a single unit cell
questions.exp: If both lattices have the same lattice parameter a, the unit cell volume = a³ is identical for both. However, atomic radius, packing factor, and number of atoms per unit cell are all different for FCC (4 atoms, APF=0.74, R=a√2/4) vs BCC (2 atoms, APF=0.68, R=a√3/4).

questions.text: In a BCC unit cell, the body-center atom has 8 nearest neighbours (the corner atoms). How many nearest neighbours does a corner atom have?
questions.exp: In BCC, each corner atom is shared by 8 unit cells. Each unit cell has one body-center atom that is a nearest neighbor. So each corner atom has nearest neighbors from 8 surrounding unit cells → 8 nearest neighbors. BCC coordination number = 8.

questions.text: The number of atoms per unit cell for a BCC structure:
questions.exp: BCC unit cell: 8 corner atoms × 1/8 each = 1, plus 1 body-center atom × 1 = 1. Total = 2 atoms per unit cell.

questions.text: What is the fraction of the volume occupied by atoms in a BCC unit cell?
questions.exp: The atomic packing factor (APF) for BCC = (2 × (4/3)πR³)/a³. In BCC, R = a√3/4. Substituting: APF = 2 × (4/3)π × (a√3/4)³/a³ = π√3/8 ≈ 0.680. So 68% of the volume is occupied by atoms.

questions.text: What is the shortest distance between two atoms of a close-packed cubic lattice of lattice parameter a?
questions.exp: FCC is the close-packed cubic lattice. Atoms touch along [110] (face diagonal = a√2). Nearest neighbor distance = 2R = a√2/2 = a/√2.

questions.text: How many octahedral interstices are there in an FCC unit cell?
questions.exp: FCC has 4 atoms per unit cell and 4 octahedral interstices per unit cell: 1 body-center site + 12 edge-center sites × 1/4 each = 1 + 3 = 4. So there are 4 octahedral interstices per FCC unit cell.

questions.imgAlt: Figure 2: FCC unit cell with lattice parameter a. Points O, S, V, R label corners; U is the midpoint of a vertical edge; X is the face-centre on the top-right face.
questions.text: Figure 2 shows an FCC unit cell with lattice parameter a. If UX = XV, find the planar density of the plane parallel to OSVR passing through X.
questions.exp: The plane through X parallel to OSVR is a (200) mid-plane of the FCC cell. It intersects the centres of exactly 4 face-centred atoms (on the 4 surrounding faces). Each contributes ¼ of its area to the a×a square cross-section, giving a total of 1 equivalent atom. Planar density = 1/a² = a⁻².

questions.imgAlt: Five diagrams (i)-(v) showing different atomic arrangements: (i) one large circle centred with 4 quarter-circles at corners, (ii) four circles touching at corners of a square with gaps at centre, (iii) single large circle filling the square, (iv) two half-circles top and bottom, (v) four pointed-star white gap with atoms at corners.
questions.context:
📋 Reference for Q5 (2010)
The (200) mid-plane of FCC contains 4 face-centred atoms arranged at the corners of a square. These atoms touch each other along the face diagonals.
questions.text: Which diagram correctly shows the atomic arrangement of the plane from Q4?
questions.exp: The 4 face-centred atoms on the (200) cross-section touch each other along the face diagonals of the perpendicular faces. This creates 4 equal circles arranged at the corners of a square, each touching its two neighbours — exactly as shown in diagram (ii).

questions.imgAlt: Figure 2: FCC unit cell. Plane SXR passes through corner S, face-centre X, and corner R.
questions.text: In Figure 2, what are the Miller indices of the plane SXR?
questions.exp: From the geometry of Figure 2 the plane SXR intercepts the x-axis at a, the y-axis at a/2, and the z-axis at a/2. Taking reciprocals: 1, 2, 2. Parentheses denote a plane, giving (122). Square brackets would denote a direction.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a crystalline defect?
questions.exp: A grain is a continuous, perfectly ordered single-crystal region within a polycrystalline material. It is the fundamental ordered structure — not a defect. The grain boundary (2D defect), dislocations (1D line defects), and self-interstitial atoms (0D point defects) are all classified as crystalline defects.

questions.text: Which defect(s) form during the solidification process?
questions.exp: All three form naturally during solidification: (i) As multiple crystal nuclei grow and meet, their misoriented boundaries become grain boundaries. (ii) When crystal growth fronts collide with slight misalignment, dislocations are created. (iii) At any temperature above 0 K, thermal fluctuations leave some lattice sites vacant. All three are unavoidable consequences of solidification.

questions.imgAlt: Five atomic plane diagrams (i)-(v): (i) one large circle with four quarter-circles at corners, (ii) four touching circles at corners of a square, (iii) single large circle filling the square, (iv) two half-circles at top and bottom, (v) four-pointed star gap with atoms at corners.
questions.text: Which of the following planes passes through the body centre of a BCC unit cell?
questions.exp: The (110) plane is a diagonal plane that cuts through two opposite vertical edges of the cube and passes directly through the geometric body-centre. In BCC, this plane contains 2 corner atoms (4 × ¼ shared = 1) plus the body-centre atom, giving the 4-circle touching arrangement shown in diagram (ii). Diagram (ii) is the (110) plane of BCC.

questions.text: Which of the following is/are usually amorphous at room temperature?
questions.exp: Glass is the textbook example of an amorphous solid — it has no long-range crystalline order. Liquid water also lacks long-range crystalline order and is amorphous. Diamond is a perfectly crystalline covalent solid with a well-defined crystal structure. Therefore only water and glass qualify.

questions.imgAlt: Three atomic plane diagrams: (i) one large circle at centre with four quarter-circles at corners — SC(100) type, (ii) four circles touching at corners — FCC/BCC(110) type, (iii) diamond/star pattern with four atoms at corners — another plane type. Options iv and v are text: 'All close packed' and 'None close packed'.
questions.text: Which of the following planes is/are close packed?
opts: All of the above are close packed
None of the above are close packed
questions.exp: Close-packed means atoms physically touch along every direction within the plane. Diagram (i) resembles SC(100) where corner atoms do NOT touch (gap between them). Diagram (ii) looks like (110) of BCC where atoms touch in one direction but not the other. Diagram (iii) shows atoms only at corners with large gaps. None of these planes satisfies the strict close-packed criterion.

questions.text: What are the Miller indices of a plane intersecting x, y, z at 0.25a, 0.5a, 0.75a?
questions.exp: Take reciprocals of the intercepts in units of a: 1/0.25 = 4, 1/0.5 = 2, 1/0.75 = 4/3. To clear fractions multiply all by 3: 12, 6, 4. Miller indices of a plane are always enclosed in parentheses (not square brackets), giving (12 6 4). Option (iii) uses commas and is non-standard notation.

questions.text: Which of the following planes is close packed?
opts: None of the above is close packed
questions.exp: A close-packed plane requires atoms to touch in ALL directions within it. SC(100): corner atoms do not touch (a > 2R for SC). BCC(110): atoms touch along [111] body diagonal direction but NOT along the [001] edge direction. BCC(111): only 2 atoms and they do not touch. FCC(110): atoms touch along [110] but NOT along [001]. The truly close-packed plane in FCC is (111) — but that option is not listed. Therefore none of these are close packed.

questions.text: Which of the following directions is close packed?
opts: None of the above is close packed
questions.exp: In simple cubic, a = 2R, so nearest-neighbour atoms sit at distance a = 2R along [100] — meaning they are touching. Therefore [100] of SC is a close-packed direction. BCC close-packed direction is [111] (body diagonal). FCC close-packed direction is [110] (face diagonal), not [111]. [100] of BCC has atoms that do not touch along the edge.

questions.text: What are the Miller indices of a plane intersecting x, y, z at 0.25a, 0.25a, 0.75a?
questions.exp: Take reciprocals: 1/0.25 = 4, 1/0.25 = 4, 1/0.75 = 4/3. Multiply through by 3 to clear fractions: 12, 12, 4. Divide by common factor 4: 3, 3, 1. Enclose in parentheses for a plane: (3 3 1).

questions.text: What is the planar density of the (110) plane of BCC, if the atomic radius is R?
questions.exp: The (110) plane of BCC contains 2 equivalent atoms (4 corner × ¼ + 1 centre = 2). The area of this rectangular plane is a × a√2. For BCC, a = 4R/√3, so a² = 16R²/3 and area = a²√2 = 16√2R²/3 ≈ 7.54R². Planar density = 2/7.54R² ≈ 1/3.77R² ≈ 1/3.8R².

questions.imgAlt: Figure 1: Cubic unit cell with origin at bottom-left-front. Five points (i)-(v) on edges, each a/3 from nearest corner. Point (i) on x-axis, (ii) on y-axis at back-left, (iii) on z-axis at top, (iv) on the top-right-front area, (v) on the y-axis front.
questions.context:
📋 Figure 1 Reference for Q40–Q42 (2017)
All five points are on edges at distance a/3 from the nearest corner. Origin is at bottom-left-front. X-axis points toward viewer, Y points right, Z points up.
questions.text: In Figure 1, which point when connected to the origin shows direction [302]?
questions.exp: Direction [302] has components x:y:z = 3:0:2. Normalize so the largest component hits the cell boundary: scale by a/3, giving (a, 0, 2a/3). This is a point on the XZ face at x = a, z = 2a/3, i.e. a/3 below the top-front edge. From the figure this is point (iv).

questions.imgAlt: Figure 1: Cubic unit cell with five labelled edge points each a/3 from nearest corner.
questions.context:
📋 Figure 1 Reference for Q40–Q42 (2017)
All five points are on edges at distance a/3 from the nearest corner. Origin is at bottom-left-front.
questions.text: In Figure 1, which point when connected to the origin shows direction [233]?
questions.exp: Direction [233] has components x:y:z = 2:3:3. Normalize so y and z = a: scale by a/3, giving (2a/3, a, a). This is the top-back-right edge corner displaced a/3 inward along x — point (ii) in Figure 1.

questions.imgAlt: Figure 1: Cubic unit cell with five labelled edge points each a/3 from nearest corner.
questions.context:
📋 Figure 1 Reference for Q40–Q42 (2017)
All five points are on edges at distance a/3 from the nearest corner. Origin is at bottom-left-front.
questions.text: In Figure 1, which point when connected to the origin shows direction [023]?
questions.exp: Direction [023] has x = 0, y:z = 2:3. Normalize so z = a: scale by a/3, giving (0, 2a/3, a). This lies on the top-back-left edge, displaced a/3 from the top-back corner along y — point (iii) in Figure 1.

questions.imgAlt: Figure 2: BCC-like cubic lattice with one atom in the middle connected to 8 corner atoms.
questions.context:
📋 Figure 2 Reference for Q51 (2018)
BCC-type structure: atom in the middle with 8 nearest neighbours at the corners. Side length a = 2 nm.
questions.text: Find the number of atoms per square metre of the (110) plane of the structure in Figure 2. Side length = 2 nm.
questions.exp: Figure 2 is a BCC structure. The (110) plane contains 2 equivalent atoms (4 corners × ¼ + 1 body-centre = 2). Area = a × a√2 = 2×10⁻⁹ × 2√2×10⁻⁹ = 5.657×10⁻¹⁸ m². Planar density = 2 / 5.657×10⁻¹⁸ ≈ 3.54×10¹⁷ ≈ 3.5×10¹⁷ atoms/m².

questions.imgAlt: Figure 3: Cubic unit cell with origin at bottom-left-front. Five points (i)-(v) on edges, each a/4 from nearest corner.
questions.context:
📋 Figure 3 Reference for Q53–Q56 (2018)
All five points are on edges at distance a/4 from the nearest corner. Origin is at bottom-left-front. X points toward viewer, Y points right, Z points up.
questions.text: In Figure 3, which point when connected to the origin shows direction [304]?
questions.exp: Direction [304] has x:y:z = 3:0:4. Normalize so z = a: scale by a/4, giving (3a/4, 0, a). This point is on the top-front edge, a/4 from the top-front-left corner — point (iv) in Figure 3.

questions.imgAlt: Figure 3: Cubic unit cell with five labelled edge points each a/4 from nearest corner.
questions.context:
📋 Figure 3 Reference for Q53–Q56 (2018)
All five points on edges at a/4 from nearest corner.
questions.text: In Figure 3, which point when connected to the origin shows direction [443]?
questions.exp: Direction [443] has x:y:z = 4:4:3. Normalize so x and y = a: scale by a/4, giving (a, a, 3a/4). This is on the top-right-back edge, a/4 below the top corner — point (ii) in Figure 3.

questions.imgAlt: Figure 3: Cubic unit cell with five labelled edge points each a/4 from nearest corner.
questions.context:
📋 Figure 3 Reference for Q53–Q56 (2018)
Point (ii) is located at approximately (a/4, 0, 3a/4) from the origin (on the left-front edge, a/4 from the top-front corner).
questions.text: In Figure 3, which point when connected to point (ii) shows direction [010]?
questions.exp: Direction [010] points purely in the +Y direction. Starting from point (ii) on the left-front edge and moving only in +Y leads to the equivalent point on the right-front edge at the same height — point (v) in Figure 3, which shares the same x and z coordinates but is displaced by a in Y.

questions.imgAlt: Figure 3: Cubic unit cell with five labelled edge points each a/4 from nearest corner.
questions.context:
📋 Figure 3 Reference for Q53–Q56 (2018)
Point (ii) is on the left-front edge at a/4 from the top corner.
questions.text: In Figure 3, which point when connected to point (ii) shows direction [110]?
questions.exp: Direction [110] has equal +X and +Y components, zero Z. From point (ii) moving equally in +X and +Y (diagonally across the top face) leads to point (iv) — the point on the top-right-front edge at the same z-height as (ii).

questions.imgAlt: Figure 4: Cubic unit cell with labelled corners. O is the origin at front-left-bottom. A is front-left-top (along X), B is front-right-bottom (along Y), C is far right (along Y), D is top-right-back, E is top-left-back, F is back-left-bottom, G is back-right-bottom. Z axis points up from E/D.
questions.context:
📋 Figure 4 Reference for Q57–Q61 (2018)
Cubic unit cell. O = origin (front-left-bottom). A = (a,0,0) along X. B = (0,a,0) along Y. C = far corner on Y-Z face. D = top-right-back. E = top-left (on Z axis). F = back-left-bottom. G = back-right-bottom.
questions.text: In Figure 4, what are the Miller indices of plane AEC?
questions.exp: Plane AEC passes through three corners of the unit cell. Identifying their coordinates: A=(a,0,0), E=(0,0,a), C=(0,a,0) — each intercept is exactly a on one axis. Reciprocals: 1/1, 1/1, 1/1 = (111). This is the standard 111 close-packed plane of FCC.

questions.imgAlt: Figure 4: Labelled cubic unit cell with corners O, A, B, C, D, E, F, G.
questions.context:
📋 Figure 4 Reference for Q57–Q61 (2018)
O = origin. A=(a,0,0), B=(0,a,0), C far on Y, D=top-right-back, E=(0,0,a), F=back-left, G=back-right.
questions.text: In Figure 4, what are the Miller indices of plane AGC?
questions.exp: Plane AGC passes through A=(a,0,0), G=(0,a,0) on the bottom and C=(0,a,a) on the far edge. The intercepts are x=a (+1), y is parallel so ∞ in one sense — actually tracing the plane: it intersects x at +a and z at -a (negative z intercept) giving reciprocals (1, 0̄, 1) — more carefully the plane cuts x=+1, y=∞, z=-1 giving indices (11̄1).

questions.imgAlt: Figure 4: Labelled cubic unit cell.
questions.context:
📋 Figure 4 Reference for Q57–Q61 (2018)
Plane ABDE contains four labelled corners.
questions.text: In Figure 4, what are the Miller indices of plane ABDE?
questions.exp: Plane ABDE contains points A=(a,0,0), B=(0,a,0), D=(0,a,a), E=(0,0,a). This plane intercepts x at +a and y at +a but is parallel to z (no z intercept → z intercept = ∞). Reciprocals: 1/1, 1/1, 1/∞ = 1, 1, 0. Miller indices = (110).

questions.imgAlt: Figure 4: Labelled cubic unit cell. BCC structure assumed.
questions.context:
📋 Figure 4 Reference for Q60 (2018)
BCC structure. Close-packed direction in BCC is [111] — the body diagonal.
questions.text: If Figure 4 is BCC, which direction is close packed?
questions.exp: In BCC the close-packed direction is [111] — the body diagonal along which atoms touch: corner → body-centre → opposite corner. OD connects O=(0,0,0) to D=(a,a,a), which is exactly the [111] body diagonal. Therefore OD is the close-packed direction.

questions.imgAlt: Figure 4: Labelled cubic unit cell. FCC structure assumed.
questions.context:
📋 Figure 4 Reference for Q61 (2018)
FCC structure. Close-packed direction in FCC is [110] — the face diagonal.
questions.text: If Figure 4 is FCC, which direction is close packed?
questions.exp: In FCC the close-packed direction is [110] — the face diagonal along which atoms touch. EDB: E=(0,0,a), D=(a,a,a), B=(0,a,0). Vector ED = (a,a,0) which is [110], and vector DB = (-a,0,-a) = [-101]. These are two different directions so EDB as a path follows [110] then [-101] — but examining the path E→D→B, the segment E→D is [110] which IS the FCC close-packed direction. The correct answer is (iii) EDB following the [110] face diagonal.

questions.text: In which hardness testing method(s) is it required to measure the dimensions of the indentation?
questions.exp: Vickers and Brinell hardness are both calculated from the size (area) of the indentation — you must measure the diagonal(s) of the impression under a microscope. Rockwell hardness is measured directly from the depth of penetration using a dial gauge, so no measurement of indentation dimensions is needed.

questions.text: Indentation test can be used to:
questions.exp: Indentation tests (Brinell, Vickers, Rockwell, Knoop) measure hardness — resistance to permanent indentation. They can be applied to any sufficiently stiff material including metals and wood. They do NOT directly measure toughness or strength (though hardness correlates with strength for metals).

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a measure of a metal's ductility?
opts: All of the above are measures of ductility
questions.exp: Area under the stress-strain diagram measures TOUGHNESS (energy absorbed per unit volume to fracture), not ductility. Ductility is specifically measured by: plastic strain at fracture, percentage elongation (%EL), and percentage reduction in area (%RA).

questions.text: Ductile-Brittle Transition Temperature is applicable:
opts: For both FCC and BCC metals
questions.exp: The ductile-brittle transition temperature (DBTT) is a characteristic of BCC metals (and some HCP metals). FCC metals remain ductile even at very low temperatures because their {111}<110> slip systems remain active. DBTT does not apply to FCC metals.

questions.text: Which feature is ALWAYS present in a tensile stress-strain diagram of a metal?
questions.exp: The fracture point (C) is the ONLY feature always present in a tensile stress-strain diagram — every tensile test must end in fracture. The necking point is NOT always present: brittle metals (e.g. cast iron, ceramics, very hard alloys) fracture cleanly without any visible necking or plastic deformation. A sharply defined yield point is also NOT always present — most metals show a gradual elastic-to-plastic transition requiring the 0.2% offset method.

questions.text: Which statement is correct for a material that fractured before yielding?
opts: The toughness of the material will be low
Its UTS will be less than its yield stress
questions.exp: A material that fractures before yielding is brittle. Brittle materials have little plastic deformation — the area under their stress-strain curve (toughness) is small. So the toughness is low. It's not ductile. Its UTS equals its fracture stress which is ABOVE the elastic region, not below yield.

questions.text: Elastic modulus of steel is 200 GPa. Find the strain on a steel rod for a stress of 200 MPa if yield stress is 250 MPa.
questions.exp: Since 200 MPa < 250 MPa (yield stress), deformation is elastic. ε = σ/E = 200×10⁶ / 200×10⁹ = 10⁻³ = 0.001.

questions.text: Impact test can be used to:
opts: Determine ductile-brittle transition temperature in metals
Measure the hardness of brittle materials
Determine the electrical resistance of materials
questions.exp: The Charpy and Izod impact tests measure energy absorbed during fracture. By testing at different temperatures, the ductile-brittle transition temperature can be found (energy drops sharply). Impact tests do NOT measure hardness, electrical resistance, or tensile strength directly.

questions.text: Which of the following is/are true about ductility and malleability?
opts: Ductility is related to compressive stresses
Malleability is related to tensile stresses
questions.exp: Ductility is the ability to deform under tensile stress (drawn into wires). Malleability is the ability to deform under compressive stress (hammered into sheets). All ductile materials are also malleable (ductility implies sufficient plastic deformation capacity), but not vice versa — some materials (like lead) are malleable but not ductile.

questions.text: Compressive strength of a brick is 300 MN/m². Density is 2000 kg/m³. Maximum theoretical height of a brickwork column without crushing the bottom brick? (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.exp: Stress at bottom = ρ × g × h. For max h: σ = ρgh → h = σ/(ρg) = 300×10⁶ / (2000 × 10) = 300×10⁶ / 20,000 = 15,000 m = 15 km.

questions.text: Brittle fracture is more destructive than ductile fracture because:
opts: No extra stress needed during crack propagation
questions.exp: Brittle fracture is characterized by all three: (i) it is catastrophic — sudden and without warning; (ii) cracks propagate at very high speed (close to speed of sound in the material); (iii) once initiated, the crack is self-sustaining — no additional stress is required (Griffith crack theory). All are correct.

questions.text: Plastic deformation of a crystalline metal:
opts: Is recovered when the applied load is removed
Occurs within the Hooke's Law region of a stress-strain curve
Occurs due to the movement of dislocations
Increases the elastic modulus of a material
All of the above are correct
questions.exp: Plastic deformation is permanent (not recovered), occurs OUTSIDE the Hooke's Law region, and occurs by dislocation movement (slip). It does NOT increase elastic modulus (E depends on atomic bond stiffness, not dislocation density). The correct answer is (iii) only.

questions.text: Which statement is correct about toughness?
opts: High strength + high ductility → more toughness
Low strength + low ductility → more toughness
High hardness + high strength → more toughness
High brittleness + high strength → more toughness
High brittleness + low strength → more toughness
questions.exp: Toughness = area under the stress-strain curve ≈ ability to absorb energy. A material needs BOTH high strength (high stress) AND high ductility (large strain before fracture) to have high toughness. High brittleness = low ductility = less area under curve = less toughness, regardless of strength.

questions.text: During a tensile test, a metal failed before reaching its yield stress. What can be deduced about its deformation up to fracture?
opts: Deformation is originally elastic then plastic
Deformation is originally plastic then elastic
questions.exp: If the metal failed before reaching yield stress, it never entered the plastic regime. All deformation up to fracture occurred in the elastic region (below yield). Therefore deformation was completely elastic.

questions.text: Both percent elongation and percent reduction in area in a tensile test measure a material's:
questions.exp: Both %EL (percentage elongation) and %RA (percentage reduction in area) are quantitative measures of ductility — the ability of a metal to deform plastically before fracture. More elongation and more area reduction = more ductile.

questions.text: A metal that undergoes plastic deformation ONLY during compression can be called:
questions.exp: Malleability is specifically the ability to undergo plastic deformation under compressive stress (e.g., hammering, rolling). A material that can deform plastically in compression but not in tension is malleable (not ductile). Lead is a classic example.

questions.text: The stress at which plastic deformation begins in a metal is known as:
questions.exp: The yield strength (or yield stress) is the stress level at which a material transitions from elastic to plastic deformation. Below it, deformation is recoverable; above it, permanent plastic deformation occurs.

questions.text: Using the table (A: YS=310, TS=340, εf=0.23; B: YS=100, TS=120, εf=0.40; C: YS=415, TS=550, εf=0.15; D: YS=700, TS=850, εf=0.14; E: fractures before yielding). Which material has the greatest percent reduction in area?
questions.exp: Percent reduction in area is a measure of ductility, which correlates with strain at fracture. Material B has the highest strain at fracture (0.40), indicating the greatest ductility and thus the greatest %RA.

questions.text: From the same table, which material has the greatest ductility?
questions.exp: Ductility is measured by strain at fracture. Material B has the highest strain at fracture (0.40), meaning it undergoes the most plastic deformation before fracture — it is the most ductile.

Material | Yield Str. | Tensile Str. | Strain@Frac | Frac. Str. | E
A        | 310 MPa    | 340 MPa      | 0.23        | 265 MPa    | 210 GPa
