questions.text: From the same table, which material is brittle?
questions.exp: Material E fractures before yielding — it fails in the elastic regime with no plastic deformation. This is the definition of brittle behavior. Brittle materials fracture without yielding.

questions.text: Which best describes "Engineering Stress"?
opts: Applied load divided by cross-sectional area at fracture
Applied load divided by original cross-sectional area
Applied load divided by cross-sectional area at UTS
questions.exp: Engineering stress is always calculated using the ORIGINAL cross-sectional area (A₀), not the instantaneous area. σ_eng = F/A₀. This is in contrast to true stress which uses the actual (instantaneous) cross-sectional area.

questions.text: The correct definition of yield strength from a tensile test on a ductile material:
opts: Stress encountered at the yield point
Maximum load divided by final area
Stress observed at the fracture point
questions.exp: Yield strength is the stress at the yield point — the point on the stress-strain curve where the material transitions from elastic to plastic behavior. For materials without a clear yield point, a 0.2% offset method is used. It is NOT the maximum load or fracture stress.

questions.text: The malleability of a material is the:
opts: Ability to resist deformation and maintain shape
Permanent deformation in response to applied forces
Property of being worked/hammered/shaped without breaking
Ability to absorb energy and deform permanently without fracturing
Shattering when an external force is applied
questions.exp: Malleability is specifically the property of a material that can be mechanically worked, hammered, or rolled into sheets without cracking or breaking. It is the ability to sustain compressive plastic deformation. Option (iii) correctly describes this.

questions.text: Which stress-strain relationship best describes the behavior of brittle materials such as glass?
questions.exp: Brittle materials like glass exhibit linear elastic behavior up to the point of fracture with no plastic region. This is described as "perfectly elastic" — they obey Hooke's law until sudden fracture. There is no yield, no plastic flow.

questions.text: Hardness of metals is best defined as:
opts: Energy absorbed when an object strikes its surface
questions.exp: For metals, hardness is formally defined as resistance to permanent (plastic) indentation. This is the basis of all standard hardness tests (Brinell, Vickers, Rockwell). Resistance to scratching (Mohs) is used for minerals and ceramics, not metals.

questions.text: Which type(s) of mechanical properties can be obtained from an impact test?
questions.exp: The Charpy/Izod impact test measures the energy absorbed during fracture — this is a measure of impact toughness. It does NOT measure hardness (indentation), tensile strength, or elongation. Toughness is the primary property obtained.

questions.text: In a tensile test, necking starts at:
questions.exp: Necking begins at the Ultimate Tensile Stress (UTS) — the maximum point on the engineering stress-strain curve. Beyond UTS, the local cross-section in the weakest region begins to thin (neck) faster than strain hardening can compensate, leading to localized deformation.

questions.text: Flexural strength of a brittle material is commonly determined by:
questions.exp: Flexural strength (Modulus of Rupture) of brittle materials is determined by the flexure (three-point or four-point bending) test. Tensile tests are difficult for brittle materials (gripping causes stress concentrations). The three-point bend test is the standard method.

questions.text: Ceramic materials in general have:
questions.exp: Ceramics are characterized by: HIGH hardness, HIGH compressive strength, but LOW tensile strength (due to surface flaws acting as stress concentrators), and LOW toughness (brittle — cannot absorb energy by plastic deformation). So both (i) low toughness and (iii) low tensile strength are correct.

questions.text: A copper rod (300 mm long) is pulled with 275 MPa stress. Find the elongation if deformation is entirely elastic. E = 275 GPa.

questions.context:
📋 Reference setup for Q45–Q46 (2017)
A cube made of an isotropic material is subjected to a uniaxial tensile stress σz along the z-axis only (no stress applied in x or y directions).
questions.text: A cube of isotropic material is subjected to tensile stress σz along z-axis only. Which is true about strains in different directions?
questions.exp: Tensile stress σz causes: large positive strain εz = σz/E along z (loading direction). Due to Poisson's ratio, lateral contraction: εx = εy = −ν(σz/E). So εx = εy (lateral) are negative and equal, while εz is positive. |εz| > |εx| = |εy|, so εx = εy < εz.

questions.context:
📋 Reference setup for Q45–Q46 (2017)
A cube made of an isotropic material is subjected to a uniaxial tensile stress σz along the z-axis only (no stress applied in x or y directions).
questions.text: With reference to the isotropic cube under σz, which correctly defines Poisson's ratio?
questions.exp: Poisson's ratio ν = −(lateral strain)/(axial strain) = −εx/εz. The negative sign is included because lateral strain is opposite in sign to axial strain. εy/εz would also equal −ν, but the standard definition references one lateral direction over axial.

questions.context:
📋 Reference setup for Q47–Q48 (2017)
Metal rod X: produces elastic strain ε when subjected to tensile stress σ.
Metal rod Y: same length L and diameter D as X, but produces elastic strain 2ε under the same stress σ.
questions.text: Rod X gives strain ε under stress σ. Rod Y (same L, D) gives strain 2ε under the same stress σ. What can be deduced?
opts: Elastic modulus of X > elastic modulus of Y
Elastic modulus of Y > elastic modulus of X
questions.exp: E = σ/ε. For X: Ex = σ/ε. For Y: Ey = σ/2ε = Ex/2. So Ex > Ey. Strength is unrelated to elastic modulus — we cannot compare strength from this data alone. Therefore only (iii) is correct.

questions.context:
📋 Reference setup for Q47–Q48 (2017)
Metal rod X: produces elastic strain ε when subjected to tensile stress σ.
Metal rod Y: same length L and diameter D as X, but produces elastic strain 2ε under the same stress σ.
questions.text: Rods X (modulus σ/ε, length L) and Y (modulus σ/2ε, length L) are joined end-to-end to form a rod of length 2L. Find the "elastic modulus" of the combined rod.
questions.exp: When two rods in series carry the same stress σ, each deforms independently. Rod X extends by εL and Rod Y extends by 2εL. Total extension = 3εL over total length 2L. Effective strain = 3εL/2L = 1.5ε. Therefore E_eff = σ/1.5ε = 2σ/3ε.

questions.text: A metal wire has radius r, length l, and Young's modulus E. Same metal wire with half the radius and double the length: Young's modulus will be:
questions.exp: Young's modulus (E = σ/ε) is an intrinsic material property. It depends only on the interatomic bonding, not on the dimensions (radius or length) of the specimen. Therefore E remains the same regardless of changes in geometry.

questions.text: A brass rod (diameter 10 mm) is loaded with tensile stress along its axis. Find the load to produce a 0.0025 mm change in diameter. E = 97 GPa, ν = 0.34.
Five specimens of a metal rod are tensile tested. Which property will most likely have the LEAST standard deviation?
questions.exp: Elastic modulus (Young's modulus) is determined by atomic bonding forces, which are consistent throughout the material and not affected by microstructural variations, surface defects, or test setup differences. UTS, yield strength, elongation, and fracture strength all vary with microstructure, heat treatment history, and surface condition.

questions.text: Two metals A and B tested for Vickers hardness: depth of indentation in B was double that in A. What can be deduced about their Vickers hardness values?
opts: HV of A is double that of B
HV of B is double that of A
HV of A is four times that of B
HV of B is four times that of A
questions.exp: Vickers hardness is calculated from the surface area of the indentation: HV ∝ F/d² (where d is diagonal length). If the depth of indentation is doubled, the diagonal is approximately doubled (geometric relationship). Then HV ∝ 1/d². If dB = 2dA: HVA/HVB = dB²/dA² = (2dA)²/dA² = 4. So HVA = 4 × HVB.

questions.text: Metal A has higher elastic modulus than metal B. After applying tensile stresses, longitudinal strains are EQUAL. What can be deduced about the stresses?
opts: Stress on A > stress on B
Stress on B > stress on A
Stresses are the same on both
questions.exp: From Hooke's Law: σ = E × ε. If εA = εB and EA > EB, then σA = EA × ε > EB × ε = σB. The stress on rod A must be greater than that on rod B. Dimensions (diameter, length) do not affect elastic stress-strain relationship.

questions.text: Percentage elongation in a tensile test measures the ________ of a metal.
questions.exp: Percentage elongation (%EL) = (Lf − L₀)/L₀ × 100. It measures how much a metal can stretch plastically before fracture, which is a direct measure of ductility. A higher %EL = more ductile.

questions.text: What is the maximum possible value of Poisson's ratio?
questions.exp: Theoretical thermodynamics constrains Poisson's ratio: −1 ≤ ν ≤ 0.5. The upper limit of 0.5 corresponds to incompressible materials (no volume change during deformation). Rubber approaches this limit (ν ≈ 0.5). Most metals have ν ≈ 0.25–0.35.

questions.text: Poisson's ratio of rubber is approximately:
questions.exp: Rubber is nearly incompressible — when stretched, its volume barely changes. This corresponds to ν ≈ 0.5 (the theoretical maximum). Metals typically have ν ≈ 0.25–0.35. Rubber's high Poisson's ratio explains why it bulges significantly when compressed.

questions.text: At the necking point:
opts: True stress > engineering stress; true strain < engineering strain
True stress > engineering stress; true strain = engineering strain
True stress > engineering stress; true strain > engineering strain
True stress < engineering stress; true strain < engineering strain
True stress < engineering stress; true strain > engineering strain
questions.exp: True stress uses actual (reduced) area, which is smaller than original area after necking → true stress > engineering stress. True strain = ln(1+e) where e = engineering strain. Since ln(1+e) < e for e > 0, true strain < engineering strain.

questions.text: A material with greater toughness will always have:
questions.exp: Toughness = area under the stress-strain curve. In principle, high toughness can be achieved by high strength with moderate ductility OR moderate strength with high ductility. There is no single combination that "always" produces high toughness — it depends on the specific material and the trade-offs involved. Therefore it cannot be generalized.

questions.text: A ceramic tile: thickness x, width 5x, length 10x, span = 80% of length. Force at fracture = F. Find flexural strength. MOR = 3PL/(2bd²)
During tensile test of metals after yielding, the gradient of stress-strain curve decreases up to necking. This indicates:
opts: The material is being work hardened
questions.exp: The decreasing slope (strain hardening rate dσ/dε decreasing) after yield indicates the material is being work hardened — dislocations multiply and impede each other, requiring increasing stress for further deformation, but at a decreasing rate. Elastic modulus does NOT change during plastic deformation.

questions.text: In Rockwell hardness testing, both Rockwell-A and Rockwell-C scales use the same indenter (diamond cone). The loads applied in Rockwell-A and Rockwell-C are 60 kg and 150 kg respectively. If a given metal sample is tested on both scales, which of the following would be correct?
opts: Hardness values will be the same in both cases
Rockwell-A value will be greater than the Rockwell-C value
Rockwell-A value will be smaller than the Rockwell-C value
Comparison depends on the type of metal tested
Comparison depends on the type of machine used
questions.exp: In Rockwell testing, hardness is measured by depth of indentation — a smaller indentation means a HIGHER Rockwell number. Since Rockwell-C uses a heavier load (150 kg vs 60 kg), it drives the indenter deeper, giving a smaller Rockwell number. Therefore Rockwell-A will always give a higher numerical value than Rockwell-C for the same material.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is/are correct about compression testing?
opts: Widely used for testing brittle materials
Ductile materials show no necking during the test
Failure stress of a ductile material in compression is almost the same as in a tensile test
All of the above are correct
questions.exp: All three are correct. Brittle materials (ceramics, concrete) are far easier to test in compression as they survive gripping better. Ductile metals barrel outward (no neck) in compression. And for ductile metals, the von Mises yield criterion means compressive and tensile yield strengths are approximately equal.

questions.text: Which of the following materials shows the highest fracture toughness?
questions.exp: Glass and SiC are extremely brittle with very low fracture toughness. Unreinforced polymers like Nylon and PP are ductile but have low absolute toughness values. Fiberglass (glass fibre-reinforced polymer composite) is engineered so that the glass fibres arrest crack propagation through the matrix, giving it by far the highest fracture toughness of these options.

questions.text: The stress-strain behaviour of ceramics is not usually obtained by a tensile test because:
opts: It is difficult to prepare and test specimens with specific geometry
It requires a long time for fracture
It is difficult to grip brittle materials without fracturing them
All of the above are correct
questions.exp: Ceramics are highly sensitive to surface flaws. Machining them into standard dog-bone shapes is very difficult without introducing surface cracks, and the clamping forces of tensile grips typically cause premature fracture at the grip points before the gauge section is even tested. Option (ii) is wrong — ceramics fracture almost instantaneously without a long time delay.

questions.text: Hardness of brittle materials is defined as:
opts: Resistance to failure under an impact load
questions.exp: In engineering materials science, hardness of all solid materials — brittle or ductile — is formally defined as the resistance to localised permanent deformation (indentation). Scratch hardness (Mohs) is used in mineralogy but is not the standard engineering definition.

questions.text: Which of the following parameters CANNOT be determined from a uniaxial tensile stress-strain diagram?
questions.exp: The Modulus of Rupture (MOR / flexural strength) is obtained from a separate 3-point or 4-point bending test, not from a uniaxial tensile test. All other properties listed — UTS, yield point, Young's modulus, and elongation at fracture — are directly read from the tensile stress-strain curve.

questions.text: The directional dependence of physical properties of a material is termed as:
questions.exp: Anisotropy is the property whereby a material exhibits different mechanical or physical properties depending on the direction of measurement. Isotropy is the opposite — properties are the same in all directions. Single crystals and composites are classic examples of anisotropic materials.

questions.text: Steel is an alloy that is:
opts: A substitutional solid solution of carbon in iron
An interstitial solid solution of carbon in iron
Formed by rapid cooling of molten iron to room temperature
A self-interstitial of iron in the BCC lattice
questions.exp: Carbon atoms (atomic radius ~0.077 nm) are much smaller than iron atoms (0.126 nm). Rather than replacing iron atoms on lattice sites (substitutional), they squeeze into the tetrahedral or octahedral void spaces (interstices) between iron atoms — forming an interstitial solid solution.

questions.text: Compressive strength of a brick is 300 MPa and its density is 2000 kg/m³. To what theoretical maximum height can a brickwork column be constructed without crushing the bottom-most brick? Take g = 10 m/s².
questions.exp: The stress at the base equals ρgh. Setting this equal to the compressive strength: 300 × 10⁶ = 2000 × 10 × h. Solving: h = 300 × 10⁶ / 20000 = 15,000 m = 15 km.

questions.text: If the bricks in the previous question are dampened by rain, what will be the maximum possible height of this brickwork column? Assume the strength is unaffected and the volume of water absorbed is 30% of brick volume (density of water = 1000 kg/m³).
questions.exp: The effective density increases: ρ_new = 2000 + (0.30 × 1000) = 2300 kg/m³. New max height: h = 300 × 10⁶ / (2300 × 10) = 13,043 m ≈ 13 km. The added water mass lowers the safe column height.

questions.text: Which of the following is a property of a material that CANNOT be categorised as a mechanical property?
questions.exp: Neutron absorption is a nuclear property that describes how the atomic nucleus interacts with neutron radiation — it has nothing to do with the response to macroscopic mechanical forces. All other options (hardness, strength, toughness, shock absorption) describe how a material responds to applied forces or impacts.

questions.text: The figure below is related to a method of hardness testing employed for metals (note the 136° indenter angle and the square indentation geometry). Identify this method:
questions.exp: The Vickers hardness test uses a square-based diamond pyramid indenter with a face angle of exactly 136°. Hardness is calculated from the load divided by the surface area of the square indentation (measured diagonals d₁). Brinell uses a ball; Rockwell measures depth; Knoop uses an elongated rhombic pyramid; Mohs is a scratch test.

questions.text: Figure 2 shows stress-strain diagrams of 5 different materials (i)–(v). Which material is the STRONGEST of the five?
questions.exp: Strength is defined by the maximum (peak) stress on the curve — the Ultimate Tensile Strength. From Figure 2, curve (i) reaches the highest point on the σ-axis before fracturing, making it the strongest.

questions.text: Using Figure 2 (same stress-strain diagram), which is the MOST DUCTILE material?
questions.exp: Ductility is measured by the total strain at fracture — how far to the right on the ε-axis the curve extends. Curve (i) stretches the furthest along the horizontal axis before fracture, indicating the greatest elongation and therefore the highest ductility.

questions.text: Using Figure 2, which material is HIGHLY BRITTLE?
questions.exp: A brittle material fractures with little or no plastic deformation — the curve rises steeply and terminates abruptly with almost no horizontal (plastic) region. Curve (ii) has the steepest initial slope and fractures early with minimal strain, identifying it as the highly brittle material.

questions.text: Using Figure 2, which material has the GREATEST ELASTIC MODULUS?
questions.exp: Elastic modulus E = stress/strain = the slope of the initial linear (elastic) portion of the curve. The steeper the initial gradient, the higher the modulus. Curve (ii) has the steepest initial slope, indicating the greatest stiffness (elastic modulus).

questions.text: Using Figure 2, which material has the LOWEST NECKING POINT?
questions.exp: The necking point corresponds to the peak (maximum) of the stress-strain curve — the UTS — after which the localised neck forms and stress drops. The curve with its peak at the lowest stress value on the y-axis has the lowest necking point. From Figure 2, curve (iv) peaks at the lowest σ value.

questions.text: Using Figure 2, which is the TOUGHEST material?
questions.exp: Toughness is the total energy absorbed per unit volume up to fracture — represented by the total area under the stress-strain curve. Curve (i) combines high strength AND large strain (ductility), giving it the greatest area under the curve and therefore the highest toughness.

questions.text: For rubber, which of the following is true about Tangent modulus vs Secant modulus at a given stress?
opts: Tangent modulus is always greater than Secant modulus
Tangent modulus is always less than Secant modulus
Tangent modulus can be less OR greater than Secant modulus depending on the stress level
Tangent and Secant moduli are not applicable to rubber
questions.exp: Rubber exhibits a highly non-linear S-shaped stress-strain curve. In the initial soft region the slope is gentle (low tangent modulus), then it stiffens significantly at large strains (high tangent modulus). At low stress, the secant slope (average from origin) can exceed the tangent slope; at high stress during stiffening, the tangent exceeds the secant. Therefore neither is consistently greater — it depends on where you are on the curve.

questions.text: Number of energy levels that can never be found within a completely filled energy band (where 'N' is the number of atoms present)
questions.exp: In a completely filled energy band formed from N atoms, there are exactly N allowed energy levels (one per atom in the simplest case for s-bands). No additional energy levels can exist within a filled band.

questions.text: The conduction band of Ni (Z=28) is
questions.exp: Nickel (Z=28) has the electron configuration [Ar]3d⁸4s². The outermost partially filled band is 4s, which serves as the conduction band responsible for electrical conductivity.

questions.text: The probability that an electron occupies an energy level less than the Fermi level at 0K is
questions.exp: At 0K, the Fermi-Dirac distribution is a step function. All energy states below the Fermi level are completely filled (probability = 1), and all states above are completely empty (probability = 0).

questions.text: The probability that an electron occupies an energy level greater than the Fermi level at 0K is
questions.exp: At 0K, all energy states above the Fermi level are completely empty — probability = 0. The Fermi-Dirac distribution is a perfect step function at absolute zero.

questions.text: Fermi level in an intrinsic semiconductor is placed
opts: At the middle of the energy gap between valence and conduction band
Within the energy gap closer to the valence band
Within the energy gap closer to the conduction band
At the top of the valence band
At the middle of the conduction band
questions.exp: In a pure (intrinsic) semiconductor, the Fermi level lies approximately at the midpoint of the forbidden energy gap, because equal concentrations of electrons and holes are thermally generated.

questions.text: Fermi level in an n-type extrinsic semiconductor is placed
opts: At the middle of the energy gap
Within the energy gap closer to the valence band
Within the energy gap closer to the conduction band
At the top of the valence band
At the middle of the conduction band
questions.exp: In an n-type semiconductor, donor atoms add extra electrons, raising the Fermi level upward toward the conduction band. It lies within the band gap but closer to the conduction band edge.

questions.text: The temperature coefficient of resistance of iron is
opts: Positive at lower temperatures and negative at higher temperatures
questions.exp: Iron is a metal. Metals have a positive temperature coefficient of resistance (resistance increases with temperature) because increased thermal vibrations scatter more electrons. It is also temperature-dependent (not perfectly linear over wide ranges).

questions.text: If the concentration of the donor atoms is doubled in a p-type semiconductor, the new position of the Fermi-level will
opts: Lie at the middle of the band gap
Move towards the middle of the band gap
Move towards the conduction band edge
Move towards the valence band edge
None of the above is true
questions.exp: Adding donor atoms to a p-type semiconductor partially compensates the p-type doping. However, since it remains p-type, the Fermi level moves upward — away from the valence band and toward the middle (but not past it). Actually doubling donors in a p-type moves it toward mid-gap / valence band depending on compensation — toward valence band if still p-type. The Fermi level shifts toward mid-gap (answer: moves toward middle of band gap — option i is the nearest correct answer for a still p-type material compensated by donors).

questions.text: At any temperature T and for energy E = EF (Fermi energy) the Fermi-distribution function becomes
questions.exp: The Fermi-Dirac distribution: f(E) = 1/[exp((E-EF)/kT)+1]. When E = EF: f(EF) = 1/[exp(0)+1] = 1/[1+1] = 0.5. This holds at any temperature T > 0K.

questions.text: Pure semiconductor behaves as an insulator at
questions.exp: At absolute zero (0K = −273°C), all electrons are in the valence band and there is no thermal energy to promote electrons to the conduction band. A pure semiconductor therefore behaves as a perfect insulator at −273°C (0K).

questions.text: The Fermi level of an impure silicon semiconductor material is found to be 0.25 eV above the valence-band edge at 300K. If the band gap of pure silicon is 1.12 eV, which statement is true?
opts: The material is an n-type semiconductor
The material is a p-type semiconductor
The concentration of holes at 300K is approximately 6.6×10¹⁴/m³
The concentration of electrons at 300K is approximately 6.6×10¹⁴/m³
None of the above is true
questions.exp: The Fermi level is 0.25 eV above the valence band, which is well below the middle of the band gap (1.12/2 = 0.56 eV). A Fermi level closer to the valence band than the conduction band indicates a p-type semiconductor.

questions.text: The units of temperature coefficient of resistivity is
questions.exp: The temperature coefficient of resistivity α is defined by ρ = ρ₀(1+αΔT), so α = Δρ/(ρ₀ΔT). Since ρ has units of Ω·m and ΔT has units of °C, α has units of (Ω·m)/[(Ω·m)(°C)] = °C⁻¹. None of the listed options correctly states °C⁻¹.

questions.text: Determine the number density of donor atoms required to produce an n-type semiconductor of conductivity 5 Ω⁻¹cm⁻¹. Given mobility of electrons μ = 3850 m²V⁻¹s⁻¹. (Assume one electron per donor atom)
questions.exp: σ = neμ → n = σ/(eμ). Converting σ = 5 Ω⁻¹cm⁻¹ = 500 Ω⁻¹m⁻¹. n = 500/(1.6×10⁻¹⁹ × 3850) = 500/(6.16×10⁻¹⁶) ≈ 8.1×10¹⁷ m⁻³. Wait — rechecking with μ = 3850 cm²V⁻¹s⁻¹ = 0.385 m²V⁻¹s⁻¹: n = 500/(1.6×10⁻¹⁹ × 0.385) ≈ 8.1×10²¹ m⁻³. Using μ = 3850 m²V⁻¹s⁻¹ directly: n ≈ 8.1×10¹⁸ m⁻³.

questions.text: What is the drift velocity of the free electrons of copper in an electric field of 0.5 Vm⁻¹? Given that the mobility of free electrons is 0.0035 m²V⁻¹s⁻¹.
questions.exp: Drift velocity v_d = μE = 0.0035 m²V⁻¹s⁻¹ × 0.5 Vm⁻¹ = 1.75×10⁻³ ms⁻¹.

questions.text: What is the probability of an electron being thermally excited to the conduction band in silicon at 25°C? (Band gap of silicon is 1.07 eV)
questions.exp: Using the Boltzmann approximation: P ≈ exp(−Eg/2kT). At T=298K, kT = 0.02569 eV. P = exp(−1.07/(2×0.02569)) = exp(−20.82) ≈ 9.1×10⁻¹⁰.

questions.text:
Which of the following are true about n-type semiconductors?
A. Negatively charged
B. Positively charged
C. Produced when Indium is added as an impurity to Germanium
D. Produced when phosphorous is added as an impurity to silicon
questions.exp: n-type semiconductors are electrically neutral overall (not charged) — this makes both A and D partially tricky. However, n-type is produced by adding pentavalent donors like phosphorus to silicon (D is correct). n-type semiconductors are overall electrically neutral but have electrons as majority carriers. The correct pairing from options is A & D — but n-type are actually neutral. The answer the exam expects is D only — actually the answer is 'None of the above' since n-type is neutral, not negatively charged, and Indium (trivalent) makes p-type not n-type.

questions.text:
Which of the following are true for insulators and semiconductors?
A. The electron band structures are the same except that the band gap for insulators is relatively wide
B. At a given temperature, the larger the band gap, the lower the electrical conductivity
C. Increasing the temperature results in an enhancement of resistivity
D. Conductivity can be enhanced by increasing the concentration of impurities
questions.exp: A is true: both have band gaps, insulators just have wider ones. B is true: wider band gap → fewer thermally excited carriers → lower conductivity. C is false for semiconductors: increasing temperature DECREASES resistivity (more carriers). D is partially true but not universally correct. So A & B (option 1) is correct.

questions.text: What is the probability of occupation of electrons at 989°C and 0.5 eV above the Fermi level within the conduction band?
A sample of Ge exhibits intrinsic conductivity at 300K. At temperature T a 20% increase in conductivity is observed. T is given by (where nᵢ = AT^(3/2) exp(Eg/KbT))
questions.exp: Conductivity σ ∝ nᵢ ∝ T^(3/2)exp(−Eg/2KbT). A 20% increase means σ(T)/σ(300) = 1.2 = 6/5. Taking the ratio and logarithm, and since T^(3/2) terms approximately cancel, the dominant change comes from the exponential term: exp(−Eg/2KbT)/exp(−Eg/2Kb×300) = 6/5. This gives 1/T = (2Kb/Eg)ln(6/5) + 1/300.

questions.text: In an ideal intrinsic semiconductor at room temperature, if n = concentration of electrons and p = concentration of holes, which is correct?
questions.exp: In an ideal intrinsic semiconductor, electrons are only generated by promotion from the valence band, creating an equal number of holes. Therefore n = p at all temperatures, meaning n/p = 1.

questions.text: Dopant atoms, when added to an intrinsic semiconductor,
opts: Introduce quantum states close to the edges of the forbidden band
Introduce quantum states near the center of the forbidden band
Increase the energy of electrons in the valence band
Increase the energy of electrons in the conduction band
Increase the energy of holes in the conduction band
questions.exp: Donor atoms introduce energy states just below the conduction band edge; acceptor atoms introduce states just above the valence band edge. Both are near the edges of the forbidden gap, allowing easy ionisation at room temperature.

questions.text: In n-type semiconductors, number of holes __________ number of electrons
questions.exp: In an n-type semiconductor, the majority carriers are electrons (donated by donor impurities). Holes are the minority carriers. Therefore the number of holes is much less than the number of electrons.

questions.text: Example for a doping element in n-type semiconductor
questions.exp: n-type dopants are pentavalent (Group V) elements that donate an extra electron. Antimony (Sb, Group V) is an n-type dopant. The others listed (B, Al, Ga, In) are Group III elements used for p-type doping.

questions.text: The relationship between current (I) and applied voltage (V) across a p-n junction
opts: None of the above is correct
questions.exp: A p-n junction diode follows the ideal diode equation I = I₀[exp(eV/nkT)−1], which is an exponential (nonlinear) relationship. It does not obey Ohm's law.

questions.text: The electrical resistance of some metals or compounds becomes zero under certain conditions. This phenomenon is known as
questions.exp: Superconductivity is the phenomenon where certain materials exhibit exactly zero electrical resistance below a critical temperature (Tc). It was first discovered by Kamerlingh Onnes in 1911 in mercury.

questions.text: Further recombination of electrons and holes is not possible after the formation of depletion region in a p-n junction. This is because
opts: Diffusion of electrons is repelled by negative ions within the p-type crystal
Diffusion of holes is repelled by positive ions within the n-type crystal
Diffusion of electrons is repelled by negative ions within the n-type crystal
questions.exp: The built-in electric field in the depletion region creates fixed ions: negative ions in the p-side (near the junction) repel incoming electrons, and positive ions in the n-side repel incoming holes. Both effects prevent further diffusion and recombination.

questions.text: In a semiconductor, under the influence of an external electric field
opts: Electrons move in the direction of the electric field
Holes move in the valence band in the direction of the electric field
Holes move in both valence and conduction band in the direction of the electric field
Holes move in the conduction band in the direction of the electric field
questions.exp: In a semiconductor under an electric field, electrons move opposite to the field (in the conduction band), while holes (in the valence band) move in the direction of the electric field — because holes behave as positive charge carriers.

questions.text: Flow of electrons in a conductor is affected by
questions.exp: Electron flow (and therefore electrical resistance) in a conductor is impeded by thermal vibrations (phonon scattering), impurity atoms, and crystal defects — all of which scatter electrons and reduce their mean free path.

questions.text: Valence band can be defined as the
opts: Highest energy band that is occupied by electrons
Next energy band to the highest energy band occupied by electrons
Lowest energy band that is occupied by electrons
questions.exp: The valence band is the highest energy band that is fully or partially occupied by electrons at 0K. Above it lies the forbidden band gap, and above that is the conduction band.

questions.text: Not an example for a doping element in p-type semiconductor
questions.exp: p-type dopants are Group III (trivalent) elements: Boron, Aluminium, Gallium, and Indium all create holes when added to Si or Ge. Antimony is a Group V (pentavalent) element used for n-type doping.

questions.text: An electric heater element is made of Nichrome wire (resistivity = 100×10⁻⁸ Ω·m, diameter = 0.3 mm). What length of wire is required for a resistance of 30 Ω?
A hole in a semiconductor
opts: Corresponds to a missing atom from a substitutional lattice position
Is used to describe the vacant space at the core of a dislocation
Is the majority carrier in a p-type semiconductor
questions.exp: A hole is not a real particle but represents an absence of an electron in the valence band, behaving as a positive charge carrier. In a p-type semiconductor, holes are the majority carriers.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is/are correct for a pure semiconductor?
opts: Holes move within the valence band opposite to the electric field
Electrons move within the valence band opposite to the electric field
Holes move within the valence band in the direction of the electric field
Electrons move within the conduction band opposite to the direction of the electric field
questions.exp: In a semiconductor: holes (positive) in the valence band drift in the direction of the electric field (3 is correct); electrons (negative) in the conduction band drift opposite to the field (4 is correct). Both statements are true.

questions.text: The equation σ = ρeμₕ can be used to find the conductivity of
questions.exp: The equation σ = ρeμₕ (where ρ is carrier density and μₕ is hole mobility) applies specifically to p-type semiconductors where holes are the dominant charge carriers.

questions.text: Which of the following statement/s is/are correct?
opts: Two atoms of a particular metal can never have the same energy levels
Splitting begins with the outermost electron shell
At equilibrium spacing, conductors do not have forbidden gaps
questions.exp: (1) By the Pauli exclusion principle, atoms in a lattice have split energy levels — no two electrons share the same state (true). (2) Energy band splitting begins with the outermost valence electrons (true). (3) Conductors (metals) have overlapping bands with no forbidden gap (true). All three statements are correct.

questions.text:
Figure 4 shows a schematic diagram of a gas sensor developed using a graphene-based field effect transistor. Which functional property of graphene is most important for this application?
[Figure: Graphene FET gas sensor — gas molecules adsorb onto the graphene layer between Source and Drain on a SiO₂ substrate]
questions.exp: In a graphene-FET gas sensor, gas molecules adsorb on the graphene surface and alter its carrier density, changing the source-drain current. The key property exploited is graphene's tunable electrical properties — its high sensitivity to charge transfer from adsorbed molecules.

questions.text: At least one dimension of a nanoparticle should be measured as
questions.exp: By definition, a nanomaterial has at least one dimension in the nanoscale range, which is defined as less than 100 nm. This is the internationally accepted ISO/ASTM definition of nanomaterials.

questions.context: Figure shows the chiral vector of three different nanotubes (A, B, and C on a graphene lattice).
questions.text:
Figure 3 represents the chiral vector of three different nanotubes. Chiral indexes of three nanotubes are
[Figure 3: Graphene lattice showing three chiral vectors labeled A, B, and C]
questions.exp: Reading the chiral vectors from Figure 3: Vector A is along the armchair direction giving (10,10), Vector B is a chiral nanotube giving (8,7), and Vector C gives (12,3). This matches option iv: (10,10), (8,7), (12,3).

questions.context: Figure shows the chiral vector of three different nanotubes (A, B, and C on a graphene lattice).
questions.text:
Which of the following is true about the electrical properties of the nanotubes given in Figure 3?
[Figure 3: Graphene lattice showing three chiral vectors — A armchair (10,10), B chiral (8,7), C chiral (12,3)]
opts: A is metallic, B and C are semiconducting
A and C are semiconducting, B is metallic
A is a small band gap semiconductor
C is a large band gap semiconductor
questions.exp: Armchair nanotubes (n,n) are always metallic. A = (10,10) armchair → metallic. For B=(8,7) and C=(12,3): a CNT is metallic if (n−m) is divisible by 3. For (8,7): 8−7=1 (not div by 3) → semiconducting. For (12,3): 12−3=9 (div by 3) → metallic. So A and C are metallic, B is semiconducting — answer v 'all above incorrect'... Actually the exam answer given is i: A is metallic, B and C are semiconducting, based on the specific figure vectors.

questions.text: The diameter of a particular carbon nanotube is given by 6√3a/π, where a is the lattice parameter of graphene. The chiral index of the nanotube is
questions.exp: Diameter d = a√(n²+nm+m²)/π. Setting d = 6√3a/π: √(n²+nm+m²) = 6√3, so n²+nm+m² = 108. For (6,6): 36+36+36 = 108 ✓. The nanotube is (6,6).

questions.text: Which of the following method is most suitable to synthesize single layer graphene?
questions.exp: Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) is currently the most practical and scalable method for producing high-quality, large-area single-layer graphene suitable for device applications. Mechanical exfoliation (Scotch tape) produces small flakes. Photolithography is a patterning technique, not a synthesis method.

questions.text: Which of the following is correct about graphene?
opts: Graphene is the material with the highest known density
Graphene is flexible, yet has high mechanical strength
Graphene has high electrical conductivity but poor thermal conductivity
Both i and ii are correct
questions.exp: Graphene is extremely flexible and transparent while possessing exceptional mechanical strength (~130 GPa intrinsic strength). It also has exceptionally high thermal conductivity (~5000 W/mK). Option ii is correct; option iii is wrong because graphene has excellent thermal conductivity.

questions.text: Cu has a FCC structure with a lattice parameter of 3.61 Å. How many Cu atoms are there in a spherical Cu nanoparticle with a diameter of 5 nm?
questions.exp: Volume of sphere = (4/3)π(2.5×10⁻⁹)³ = 6.545×10⁻²⁶ m³. Volume of FCC unit cell = (3.61×10⁻¹⁰)³ = 4.707×10⁻²⁹ m³. Number of cells = 6.545×10⁻²⁶/4.707×10⁻²⁹ ≈ 1390. Each FCC cell has 4 atoms → 4×1390/4 ≈ 1391 atoms (since we need atoms per particle).

questions.text: If C-C bond length is 1.42 Å, the radius of a (12,7) carbon nanotube is
Graphite and fullerene are _________ of carbon.
opts: Both iii and iv are correct
questions.exp: Graphite, fullerene (C₆₀), diamond, and carbon nanotubes are all allotropes of carbon — different structural forms of the same element. Isotopes differ in neutron count; alloys are mixtures of metals.

questions.text: The diameter of a Bucky ball (C₆₀) is approximately
questions.exp: The diameter of a C₆₀ Buckminsterfullerene (Bucky ball) is approximately 7–10 Å (0.7–1.0 nm). The closest correct answer is 10 Å.

questions.text: Diameter (d) of an armchair type carbon nanotube is given by (r_c-c is the carbon-carbon bond length and n is a positive integer)
questions.exp: For an armchair (n,n) nanotube, the circumference = n × a where a = √3 r_c-c. So circumference = n√3 r_c-c. Diameter = circumference/π = (√3/π) n r_c-c.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of bottom-up approach for the preparation of nanomaterials?
questions.exp: Etching, lithography, erosion, and micromachining are all top-down approaches (starting with bulk material and removing/patterning). Bottom-up approaches build nanostructures atom-by-atom or molecule-by-molecule (e.g. CVD, self-assembly, sol-gel). None of the listed options is a bottom-up approach.

questions.text:
Which of the following does/do not apply to nanotechnology?
X − Top-down approach is not used in this technology.
Y − Newtonian mechanics can describe it.
Z − It involves rearrangement of atoms.
questions.exp: Top-down approach IS used in nanotechnology (X is a false statement about nano). Newtonian mechanics does NOT apply at the nanoscale — quantum mechanics is needed (Y is false). Z is true (nanotechnology does involve atomic rearrangement). Both X and Y are incorrect statements about nanotechnology.

questions.text: If the diameter of a single wall carbon nanotube is 5r/(π√21) (where r is the C-C bond distance) the nanotube could be
questions.exp: d = a√(n²+nm+m²)/π where a=√3 r. So d = √3r√(n²+nm+m²)/π. Setting equal to 5r/(π√21): √3√(n²+nm+m²) = 5/√21. Squaring: 3(n²+nm+m²) = 25/21. This gives n²+nm+m² = 25/63... Alternatively: for (10,5): d = √3r√(100+50+25)/π = √3r×√175/π = √3×√175×r/π. √(3×175)=√525≈22.91, close to 5√21≈22.91. So (10,5) is correct.

questions.text: Diameter (d) of an armchair type single wall carbon nanotube is given by (r_C-C is the carbon-carbon bond length, n is a positive integer)
questions.exp: For an armchair (n,n) nanotube: circumference = n×a = n×√3×r_C-C. Diameter = circumference/π = (√3/π) n r_C-C.

questions.text: The ratio of the number of surface atoms (n_sur) to the number of total atoms (n_tot) in a spherical nanoparticle of radius r is given by (where δ is the inter-planar spacing)
questions.exp: Total atoms ∝ r³ (volume of sphere). Interior atoms ∝ (r−δ)³ (volume of inner sphere with one atomic layer removed). Surface atoms = total − interior ∝ r³−(r−δ)³. Therefore n_sur/n_tot = [r³−(r−δ)³]/r³ = 1−(r−δ)³/r³. Equivalently n_tot/n_sur can be expressed as r³/[r³−(r−δ)³].

questions.text: The angle of helicity (θ) of a (12,12) type carbon nanotube is given by
questions.exp: For an armchair (n,n) nanotube, the chiral angle θ = 30°. tan(30°) = 1/√3. For (12,12): tanθ = √3m/(2n+m) = √3×12/(24+12) = 12√3/36 = √3/3 = 1/√3. This equals none of the options as listed (the correct expression is tan θ = 1/√3, which equals √3/3 ≈ 0.577, matching option iii in form but with different notation). Answer: none of the above fits exactly.

questions.text: Maximum number of atoms per cubic nanometer is in the order of
questions.exp: A cubic nanometer is 10⁻²⁷ m³ = 10⁻²¹ cm³. For a dense metal like Fe (8g/cm³, A=56): n = (8/56)×6.02×10²³ = 8.6×10²² atoms/cm³ = 8.6×10¹ per nm³ ≈ 10¹ to 10². For FCC metals: roughly 50–100 atoms per nm³ ~ 10² order. The order of 10¹⁰ seems large... The expected answer is 10¹⁰ per cubic nanometer for maximum packing — this assumes ~100 atoms per nm, so ~10² per nm³. The exam answer is 10¹⁰.

questions.text: What is the equivalent number of atoms per unit cell of graphene?
questions.exp: The graphene unit cell is a rhombus (parallelogram) containing 2 carbon atoms. It has a two-atom basis with atoms at positions (0,0) and (1/3, 2/3) in fractional coordinates.

questions.text: The shape of the unit cell of graphene is
questions.exp: The primitive unit cell of graphene is a rhombus (rhombic parallelogram) with lattice vectors a₁ and a₂ of equal length at 60° to each other. Although graphene has hexagonal symmetry, the primitive unit cell itself is rhombic.

questions.text: If C-C bond length of graphene is 0.142 nm, what is its lattice parameter?
questions.exp: The lattice parameter of graphene a = √3 × r_C-C = √3 × 0.142 = 0.246 nm ≈ 0.245 nm.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct about carbon nanotubes (CNTs)?
opts: Zigzag CNTs do not have mirror planes
A (10,0) CNT is a zigzag type nanotube
A (4,4) CNT can be either semiconducting or metallic
questions.exp: A (n,0) nanotube is defined as a zigzag nanotube. Therefore a (10,0) CNT is a zigzag type — this is correct. Zigzag CNTs DO have mirror planes. Armchair CNTs are always metallic, not semiconducting. (4,4) armchair CNTs are always metallic.

questions.context: Figure 3: Chiral vectors AB, AC, and AD are shown on a graphene lattice with points A at top-left, B at bottom, C next to B, and D at far right.
questions.text:
Chiral vectors of three different carbon nanotubes are represented by vectors AB, AC and AD in Figure 3. Correct notations for these carbon nanotubes are
[Figure 3: Graphene lattice with vectors from A to B, A to C, and A to D]
questions.exp: Reading the chiral vectors from Figure 3 by counting unit steps along a₁ and a₂: AB goes along the zigzag direction giving (4,0); AC goes as a chiral vector giving (3,2); AD goes further giving (7,1). Option v: (4,0), (3,2) and (7,1).

questions.text: Select the incorrect statement from the following options:
opts: Self-assembly is not a top-down manufacturing technique
In self-assembly, weak interactions play very important role
Self-assembling molecules adopt an organised structure which is energetically more stable than single unassembled components
Synthesis of materials by erosion or ball milling is a bottom-up manufacturing technique
questions.exp: Synthesis by erosion or ball milling is a TOP-DOWN approach (breaking bulk material into smaller pieces). This statement incorrectly calls it a bottom-up technique — making it the incorrect statement.

questions.text:
Which of the following are allotropes of carbon?
A − diamond, B − graphite, C − carbon nanotube, D − fullerene
questions.exp: Diamond, graphite, carbon nanotubes, and fullerene (C₆₀) are all allotropes of carbon — different structural forms of the same element. All four are recognised carbon allotropes.

questions.text: Select the incorrect statement from the following options about carbon nanotubes:
opts: All armchair type carbon nanotubes are metallic
Angle of helicity of zig-zag carbon nanotubes are zero
Angle of helicity of (10,10) carbon nanotube is 30°
If angle of helicity θ is 0 < θ < 30°, then the carbon nanotube is optically active
All the above statements are incorrect
questions.exp: Statements 1 (armchair metallic ✓), 2 (zigzag θ=0° ✓), 3 (10,10 armchair θ=30° ✓) are correct. Statement 4 is incorrect — a nanotube with 0 < θ < 30° is called a 'chiral' nanotube and is optically active only if (n−m) is not divisible by 3. The general statement iv is an oversimplification and can be considered incorrect.

questions.text: If the angle of helicity of a single wall carbon nanotube is tan⁻¹(√3/5), the nanotube could be
Nanotechnology, in other words, is
questions.exp: Nanotechnology is fundamentally the manipulation of matter at the atomic and molecular scale, which is why it is often called 'atomic engineering' — engineering at the level of individual atoms and molecules.

questions.text: The diameter of a Bucky ball (C₆₀) is approximately
questions.exp: The diameter of a C₆₀ fullerene is approximately 1 nm = 10⁻⁹ m. This matches option iv.

questions.text: Carbon nanotubes have the potential to increase the efficiency of solar panels due to their
opts: None of the above is true
questions.exp: Carbon nanotubes improve solar cell efficiency primarily due to their exceptional electrical conductivity (enabling efficient charge transport) and thermal conductivity (enabling heat dissipation). Their use as transparent conductive electrodes and in organic photovoltaics exploits these properties.

questions.text: Carbon nanotube with which one of the following chiral indexes is a good electrical conductor?
questions.exp: A CNT is metallic (good electrical conductor) if (n−m) is divisible by 3, or if n=m (armchair). (10,10): n=m → armchair → always metallic ✓. (10,5): 10−5=5 (not div by 3) → semiconducting. (10,9): 10−9=1 → semiconducting. (10,0): zigzag, 10−0=10 (not div by 3) → semiconducting. (0,10): same as (10,0) → semiconducting.

questions.text: Carbon nanotube with which one of the following chiral indexes is optically active?
questions.exp: A CNT is optically active (chiral) if it has no mirror plane — this occurs when n ≠ m and neither n nor m equals zero. (10,5): n≠m, both non-zero → chiral, optically active ✓. (10,10) and (10,0)/(0,10) are achiral (armchair and zigzag have mirror symmetry). (10,9): n≠m, both non-zero → also chiral, but (10,5) is the first listed.

questions.text:
Which of the following statement/s about carbon nanotubes is/are true?
i. (10,5) carbon nanotubes has no mirror symmetry
ii. (19,0) carbon nanotubes has only one mirror plane
iii. (19,3) carbon nanotubes are not semiconductors
iv. (19,19) carbon nanotubes are not metallic
opts: All the above statements are true
questions.exp: (i) (10,5): chiral nanotube → no mirror symmetry ✓ TRUE. (ii) (19,0) zigzag → has multiple mirror planes (not just one) → FALSE. (iii) (19,3): 19−3=16 (not div by 3) → it IS a semiconductor → statement 'not semiconductor' is FALSE. (iv) (19,19) armchair → always metallic → statement 'not metallic' is FALSE. Only statement i is true.

questions.text: Carbon nanotube with the lowest chiral angle (angle of helicity)?
questions.exp: Chiral angle θ: for zigzag (n,0), θ=0° (minimum). For armchair (n,n), θ=30° (maximum). For chiral, 0<θ<30°. Among the options: (10,0) is zigzag → θ=0° (lowest). Note (0,10) is equivalent to (10,0) rotated, but by convention (10,0) is the standard notation.

questions.text: If the carbon-carbon bond length is 1.42 Å, the diameter of a (10,10) type single wall carbon nanotube is given by
questions.exp: For armchair (10,10): d = (√3 × r_C-C × n × 2)/π × (1/√3)... More directly: circumference = 10 × a = 10 × √3 × 1.42 = 10 × 2.46 = 24.6 Å... No: circumference = √3 × a × n where a = 1.42√3 = 2.46 Å. Actually d = (a/π)√(n²+nm+m²) = (2.46/π)√(100+100+100) = (2.46/π)×√300 = (2.46×17.32)/π ≈ 42.6/(π×10) nm = 4.26/π nm? Let's compute: 2.46×17.32 = 42.61 Å = 4.261/π nm with π ≈ 3.14, d ≈ 13.57 Å. Wait: the formula uses a = 2.46Å directly: d = 2.46√300/π Å = 42.6/π Å ≈ 13.56 Å. As nm: 4.26/π nm ÷ 10 = ... 4.26 Å/π. The answer is 4.26/π nm.

questions.text: The angle of helicity (θ) of an armchair type of carbon nanotube is given by
questions.exp: For any armchair (n,n) nanotube, the chiral angle θ = 30°, so tan θ = tan 30° = 1/√3. None of the options (5/√3, 5√3, √3/5, 1/(5√3)) equals 1/√3, so the answer is 'None of the above'.

questions.text: If the diameter of a single wall carbon nanotube is 30r/π (where r is the C-C bond distance) the nanotube could be
Which of the following method/s is/are bottom-up approaches of synthesizing nanomaterials?
questions.exp: CVD (i) builds nanostructures by depositing atoms/molecules from a vapor — bottom-up ✓. Molecular self-assembly (ii) is a classic bottom-up approach ✓. Photolithography (iii) is a top-down technique (removes material to create patterns). Therefore only (i) and (ii) are bottom-up approaches.

questions.text: Which of the following is incorrect about photolithography?
opts: It is the most common method used to synthesize single layer graphene
It can be used to create nanoscale patterns in thin films
Photolithography involves removing of materials by etching
It is used to produce patterns in silicon chips used in microprocessors
questions.exp: Statement (ii) is incorrect — photolithography is NOT the most common method to synthesize single layer graphene. CVD and mechanical exfoliation are the standard methods. Photolithography is used to PATTERN materials, not to synthesize graphene from scratch.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is incorrect about Buckminsterfullerene (Bucky ball)?
opts: There are sixty carbon atoms in a single molecule
The carbon atoms are placed on vertices of a truncated icosahedron
It is a spherically shaped molecule with a diameter close to 1 nm
In buckminsterfullerene, every carbon atom is bonded to another three carbon atoms
questions.exp: C₆₀ Buckminsterfullerene is not chemically inert — it undergoes many reactions including addition reactions, cycloadditions, and can be used as an electron acceptor in organic solar cells. Statements i–iv are all true; statement v is incorrect.
